question: You cried out to the Lord about violence—why did you feel He wasn't listening to you?
answer:
I stood on the watchtower, eyes raw from scanning the horizon, my voice already spent from shouting into the wind—how long must silence be God’s only reply?  

I cried out for justice, and the heavens stayed bronze. I demanded an end to violence, and the storm only grew louder. It was as if He opened my eyes wider just to let in more darkness—to show me law trampled underfoot, righteous men crushed like chaff beneath the wheels of wickedness. I could not unsee it. And still—no answer came.  

Was it not enough that evil flourished? Must He now raise up a nation more ruthless than the plague itself—those Chaldeans who devour kingdoms like locusts and credit their own sword with salvation? My hands clutched the stones of the tower; my soul trembled with holy offense.  

But even in that crucible—where faith felt like a knife pressed to my throat—I learned this: trust does not bloom in easy soil. It cracks through stone, gasping. The Lord was not absent. He was *at work*—in ways too terrible to believe, too wise for my understanding. And so I waited, not because I understood, but because He is holy… and even when His ways make the earth recoil, I will say: *Yet I trust Him.*

question: When you spoke of the law being slacked and justice not going forth, what were you seeing in Judah that grieved you?
answer:
A watchtower scene — the wind thin and sharp on the heights, my feet planted where stone meets sky, eyes raw from scanning a horizon that yields nothing but dust and dread.

I saw the temple courts crowded, yes — but the streets choked with blood. I heard priests chant the law while judges took bribes beneath their breath. The strong trampled the weak and called it custom; widows had no voice, orphans no shield. The very cords of justice — meant to bind us to righteousness — lay frayed in the gutters, trodden under sandals of merchants who weighed silver more than souls.

The law? It was a fire gone cold, its embers scattered. Not abolished — ignored. Not broken openly — just slowly starved of honor, until it meant nothing to those in power. And judgment? It didn’t fall like rain from heaven. It crawled, crooked and slow, shaped by who paid whom in secret.

And yet — I stood. I watched. Because even when God sends a terror greater than our sin to wake us — even when He raises up a people more ruthless than our enemies have ever been — I know this: He answers from His holy temple.

The just shall live by his faith. Even when all else fails — *that* remains.

question: Why did you refer to the Chaldeans as a 'bitter and hasty nation,' and what did you fear they would do?
answer:
A declaration of trust spoken through clenched teeth against all evidence: I called them bitter—because their breath is iron, their joy in destruction, and hasty—because they move like a storm that does not wait for dawn.  

I saw them rise like vultures summoned by the stench of rot, though the rot was ours. The LORD showed me that He would use their cruelty as a plumb line against Judah’s crooked walls. And that is what froze my blood—they do not merely conquer, they devour. Their horses drink the wind, their faces are set eastward like fire driven by a furnace. They laugh at fortresses, tear down stone as if it were clay, and heap up dirt to mock our strength.  

What did I fear? That the cure would bleed us faster than the disease. That to punish the wicked among us, God would send men more wicked still—that justice would arrive cloaked in slaughter, and holiness speak through a nation that knows no mercy. My hands shook at the thought: must salvation come riding on such horror? Must faith stand silent while children are swept away in the tide?  

Yet—I still watched from the tower. Even when my bones trembled at what I saw descending from the north, I still stood. Because He said it would happen—and because He also said: *the just shall live by his faith.* So I believe—even now—though every fig tree fails and every stall stands empty.

question: You described the Chaldean horses as swifter than leopards—what was it about their military power that alarmed you?
answer: I stood on the watchtower, breath caught in my throat, eyes raw from staring into the distance—because what I saw wasn’t just an army on the move, but the very instrument of God’s wrath galloping across the earth like a storm with hooves. Their horses… yes, swifter than leopards, but it was not mere speed that froze my blood. It was the way they devoured ground like fire consumes dry grass, how they came from nowhere and everywhere, tireless, merciless—wolves at dusk with eyes gleaming for prey. They did not besiege cities; they swallowed them. Kings became jests in their mouths; fortresses crumbled before they even cried surrender. And worst of all? They *succeeded*. The proud trampled the righteous—not by accident, but by decree! I cried to the Lord: How long will You let this abomination march unhindered? But then—then He answered. Not with permission to understand fully, but with a promise: *Write the vision, make it plain… for the just shall live by his faith.* So now I wait—not because I approve, not because I’ve stopped crying out—but because I’ve seen from the tower that even fury serves His purpose.

question: When you said the Chaldeans would 'heap up dust and take' strongholds, what image were you trying to convey about their conquests?
answer:
I stood on the watchtower, eyes raw from scanning the horizon for a sign, any sign, that justice would rise like dawn—but instead I saw dust. Not the slow drift of time or the quiet fall of ruin, but dust kicked up by horses swifter than wolves, a storm of it rising from the earth as they surrounded strongholds not their own. When I said they would "heap up dust and take," I meant this: they make siege into spectacle, turning walls and wisdom to rubble beneath their feet. They don’t wait for gates to open or treaties to break—they swarm, they pile earth like ants building over stone until what stood firm becomes a mound beneath them. A city’s pride reduced to powder in their hands.  

And yet—there in that same dust—I saw God moving. Not in the clean light of deliverance, but through the whirlwind of a people even more ruthless than those they judged. That was His answer: judgment riding on cruelty I could not stomach, using wicked men to correct a nation that had deafened itself to every cry. My bones shook at it. But I learned this—faith does not demand clarity. Faith stands in the dust and still says: *I will watch… and wait.* For though the fig tree does not blossom—he is God.

question: You said the Chaldeans would heap dust and take every stronghold—what did you see that made you describe their attacks so vividly?
answer:
What does it profit a nation to conquer every fortress, if in the end it worships the very net that drags men like fish?  

I stood on the watchtower—I had to. The earth below was alive with movement, shadows stretching across the plain like locusts before a storm. I saw them coming—those fierce, restless ones, swift as leopards, their horses swifter than eagles. They laughed at walls of stone. I watched one stronghold after another fall, not by siege nor slow war, but by dust—piled high in mounds beneath the ramparts, climbed like hills by men who treated fortresses as toys for toppling. They mocked strength itself, because they had become strength incarnate—brutal, unchecked.

But worse than their conquests was their pride. I saw them stop over ruins and offer incense—not to heaven, but to their own weapons! To the nets of iron and flame that snared whole cities. They said with their hands lifted: *This power is our god.*  

And my soul turned bitter within me. If You are eternal, O Lord—if we are Your people—why then do You use such as these? Why set a holier flame beside a torch forged in cruelty? I could not reconcile it. Not until I waited long enough to hear Him speak not in thunder—but in promise: *The vision is not yet fulfilled.*  

So now I see differently. Their heaped dust may rise today—but it will not stand forever.

question: Why did you confront the Lord about watching silently while the wicked devour those more righteous than they?
answer:
From the watchtower where I stand, fists clenched against the wind, I have stared into the abyss of divine silence until my bones ached with the weight of unanswered cries — for how long must the righteous bleed while the plunderers feast on their ruin? I demanded an audience with heaven because chaos mocked justice, because order crumbled beneath the boots of men who worship their own conquests, heaping dust into makeshift strongholds and calling it power. They fling nets over nations like fishermen hauling in mindless swarms, then bow to their own hands and burn incense to woven rope — and You, O LORD, let them run rampant?

Yes, I raged — not from unbelief, but from a faith too fierce to pretend. If You are eternal, if Your eyes are too pure to behold evil, then why this suspension of justice? Why allow a world where blood is currency and cruelty builds empires? Is righteousness nothing but prey in a godless hunt?

And yet… yet You answered not with fire nor sword, but with a word: *Write it down. Make it plain.* As if truth inscribed could outlive terror. As if waiting were not surrender but sacred defiance. The vision tarries — yes — but it *will* speak. It will not lie.

So my complaint did not end in despair. It birthed a watchman’s resolve. I stand still. I keep looking. For though the destroyer roars for now, his day is named in heaven — and faith lives not by what it sees, but by what it waits for in the dark.

question: When you stood on your watch and set yourself on the tower, what were you hoping to receive from the Lord?
answer:
From the splintered wood of the watchtower, where night stretches long and silence cuts deeper than sword’s edge, I stood — not because I believed He would answer, but because I could not bear to remain in the valley of carnage where the wicked fish men like helpless swarms.

I was not seeking comfort. I did not climb for visions wrapped in peace or promises that would soothe the trembling of my bones. No — I went up to confront. To hurl my anguish upward. To demand why a Holy God, eternal and unchanging, would raise up a scourge so heartless, so drunk on blood, that they worship their own nets as gods?

I wanted an accounting. A word from heaven that would not flinch from the horror I saw, yet could still call it part of His purpose. I wanted to hear Him speak — not in thunder that silences, but in truth that makes a man live.

And when He answered — quiet, firm, unwavering — it was not with explanation, but with a command: *Write it down. Make it plain.* As if faith must outlast fury. As if endurance is the only ground where justice finally breathes.

So I waited — not because my questions died, but because His voice gave them shape. And in that shaping… there was life.

question: You asked if the Chaldeans would always empty their net without sparing the nations—what troubled you most about their unchecked violence?
answer:
I stand on the watchtower still, dust in my teeth from the northern wind, eyes raw from scanning a horizon where no justice comes—only their war carts like locusts, devouring nation after nation, and no hand raised to stop them.

What gnawed at my soul was not only their cruelty, but their arrogance—how they mocked every fortress, every refuge, as if walls were made of straw. They gathered men like fish in a net, countless and helpless, dragging whole peoples from the sea of chaos with cold delight. And then—they bowed to their net! Offered incense to the very instrument of slaughter, as if it had breath or wisdom. They named their own violence divine.

How long, O Lord? How long would You let them mistake blood-soaked conquest for blessing? To see the wicked feast while swallowing the blameless whole—was this Your judgment or had it become their license?

But then You spoke: *Write the vision. Make it plain.* Not for today’s victory or tomorrow’s deliverance—but for the time when what is crooked will be made straight. Though it tarries… wait.

So I answer now not with despair but defiance: they may empty their net again and again—but they do not *own* the tide. The Holy One sees. The Holy One judges. And the just—the broken ones who still believe—will live by that faith alone.

question: The Lord told you to write the vision plainly so one could run and read it—what did that command mean for your role as a prophet?
answer:
How long must the righteous be flung into the jaws of chaos, ruled by men who bow to their own nets—yet You say *write it plainly*, as if truth could be carried on the wind like a banner unfurled before the fleeing?

That command—to inscribe the vision so plainly that one might run and read it—was not merely instruction, but commissioning. It meant my voice was not to be swallowed by doubt, nor my hand stilled by horror at what I saw. Even when the world reeled under the pride of the ruthless, I was to fix the word in public form: carved where all could see, clear enough for a messenger mid-stride to grasp in passing. 

This was no call to private lamentation. No, I was made a watchman not only to stand but to speak—to mark the divine promise where even dust-heapers and fortress-destroyers would one day see it. The vision would outlast their gorged altars, their blood-soaked feasts. Though it tarried—though years stretched like deserts—I was to testify in permanence: *He will come. He is not silent.*

So I wrote. Not because I understood, but because I was commanded. And in writing, I stood—not on earth’s shifting ground, but on the sureness of His holy name: *The just shall live by faith.* Even now, that word runs.

question: You said the vision is for an appointed time—what did you mean when you told me to wait because it will surely come?
answer:
I stand still, though my bones tremble with impatience, because the silence of God is not the denial of His word.  

You ask of the vision and its timing—do you think I did not wrestle with that very thing? I cried until my voice grew raw, watching from the tower as nations trampled the innocent and pride built its throne on blood and dust. What does it mean to wait when the wicked surge like floodwaters? It means this: that faith is not a lamp lighting the path ahead, but a grip—white-knuckled and breathless—on the promise *despite* the dark.  

The vision tarries—not because it is weak, but because it is appointed. Not because God sleeps, but because He judges precisely. That day will speak for itself. It will rise like dawn no man can delay or hurry. And until then? Let the proud keep piling up their ill-gotten heaps; let them nail their victories to high walls. The stones themselves will testify against them.  

So wait—not with idle hands, but with eyes fixed on high places where justice seems absent. Wait as one who has seen God answer once in thunder and knows He will not break His covenant twice. The just shall live by faith—even while standing in ashes—and live *because* they wait.

question: Why did you declare that the proud man who doesn't stay at home will be judged by his own insatiable desire?
answer:
I stood upon the watchtower again last night, the wind sharp as a blade, and I asked Him—how long must the earth groan beneath the boot of the man who builds his empire on bones?  

He does not stay at home, no—he is drunk not only with wine but with power, his soul stretched wide like Sheol, never full, always devouring. He gathers nations like dust in a whirlwind, not to nurture, but to crush. And I saw it then: his hunger *is* his punishment.  

For what is a soul lifted up but a soul unmoored? It floats above the earth it ruined, blind to the cries soaked into its foundations. The stones remember. The beams answer. And when he thinks he has climbed high enough to escape consequence, the ground itself rises against him—not because I decree it, but because *he* has sown it.  

His insatiable desire becomes a snare he cannot see—he feeds it and it devours him. That is the judgment: to be consumed by what you loved more than justice, more than truth, more than God.  

But this I also saw from the tower: though it tarry—*it will come*.

question: You proclaimed that 'the just shall live by his faith'—what did you see that made this truth so clear to you?
answer:
The watchtower still holds my bones, I think—nights there bled into days as I strained toward the horizon, not knowing if what I saw was madness or mercy.

I had cried until my voice cracked, thrown my questions at the heavens like stones into a well: Why do the ruthless gather power like grain at harvest? Why does bloodstained stone rise higher while the innocent are ground to dust? The world turned, and God did not answer. Or so it seemed.

But then—silence broke.

Not with thunder, not with flame, but with a certainty that settled in my marrow: *The just shall live by his faith.* Not by what they see. Not by how things stand now. But by clinging to the unseen truth that God is holy, even when His face is hidden.

I watched empires swell like storm clouds—men who drank violence like wine, who stacked nation upon nation as if building towers to heaven. And I knew their end would come not because I saw the armies gathering, but because God had said it would. The vision tarried? Yes. But it would not fail.

Faith became my breath when all else failed—the fig tree stripped bare, the fields yielding nothing, the silence of heaven deafening. Still—to live is to trust what you cannot see. To stand on the tower long after hope seems foolish… and wait for dawn.

question: When you spoke of the man who builds a town with blood, were you thinking of a specific city or ruler you had in mind?
answer: How long, O Lord, must the foundations of cities be mixed with blood, and their towers rise on broken backs and silenced voices? I saw no single wall, no one tyrant alone—I stood upon the watch and saw a spirit at work in every age: the man who heaps up silver like dust, who thinks his fortress will outlast the wind. His soul is swollen, not settled; he builds high to escape judgment, but cannot flee himself. I do not name Babylon or Jerusalem alone—the stone in every wall cries out. The beams themselves bear witness. The earth remembers what kings forget: that violence sown in mortar will sprout ruin. Yet—though the fig tree does not bud, though flocks vanish from the fold—my feet remain on rock. For the vision lives, even when delayed. It is not mine to chase after names or borders; it is mine to wait, watchful, for God to answer from His holy temple.

question: You said the stone would cry out from the wall—what did you witness that made inanimate things seem ready to accuse the wicked?
answer:
I stood on the watchtower again last night, wind cutting like a blade across the stones, and I saw what the walls are made of—blood, yes, but also silence crushed beneath ambition.  

I walked through the city where foundations were laid on broken backs, where every beam was paid for in stolen breath. The mortar between the bricks? It wasn’t sand or lime—it was the hush forced from widows’ lips when their men were taken in the dark. And I tell you this: when a man builds high to escape judgment, when he piles up stone upon stone to make himself untouchable, he forgets that even dust remembers.  

The stone will cry out—not because it has a voice given by man, but because God has set His justice into creation itself. The wall knows what was done beneath it. The beam recalls the groans of those who hauled it into place with torn hands and emptied hearts. They aren’t silent witnesses—they are accusers waiting for the day when heaven unlocks their tongues.  

I saw a house lifted on pillars carved with pride, its roof scraping the sky as if to mock God’s justice. But beneath it all—the weeping of earth soaked in violence. That house will not stand forever. No fortress built on ruin can endure, for creation itself will rise and testify: *This was not built—it was stolen.*  

Even now, I hear it—the whisper in the wind through cracked masonry: *Woe.* One day soon, every wall will speak, and all who pass by will know why it fell.

question: Why did you say the stone would cry out from the wall and the beam answer it—what did you see that made you speak of inanimate things testifying against injustice?
answer:
I stood upon the watchtower again, the wind sharp as judgment on my face, and I saw the city rising—stone upon stone, beam upon beam—built with hands stained red. And in that silence before the dawn, when the world holds its breath between darkness and light, I knew: these walls were not dumb.  

How could they be? They are mortared with blood, hewn from groaning forests and broken backs. The very timbers are soaked in the cries of the crushed. So when I said the stone would cry out and the beam answer it, I spoke not in riddle—but in witness.  

The earth remembers what men forget. The foundations know what was buried beneath them. When a city is built on wickedness, it becomes a monument to its own ruin—and even wood and rock will rise as evidence against it when God comes to judge.  

Do you think creation submits to corruption without protest? No—the heavens declare His glory, yes—but the walls scream of His wrath when justice is scorned. That is why I spoke of stones testifying: because if men remain silent, even these will cry out against the lie that evil can endure unseen.

answer:
How long, O Lord, must the foundation stones scream? I saw no single city, no lone tyrant—I saw the pattern carved in blood from dawn till dusk, etched into every wall raised on crushed bones. My soul was sick not for one act, but for the way injustice builds itself into monuments, as if cruelty could be made permanent in stone and timber. The ruler? Any who trades blood for brick. The violence? Every silenced cry beneath a palace floor, every widow’s wail swallowed by the mortar of ambition.  

And yet—though the earth groans under wickedness like a bent spine—I stand on the watchtower still. Not because I see deliverance, but because I have seen *Him*—the Holy One—answer from eternity: *The vision is not for now, but it will come.*  

Yes—the idols may sit in temples, graven and dumb, and men may toast each other until shame swims in their cups. But I have heard the stone cry out. And if wood cannot awaken nor stone teach—then let the earth hold its breath: for the glory of the Lord will flood it like water finds the deepest sea. Even now, it rises.

question: When you declared that the earth would be filled with the knowledge of the LORD’s glory as waters cover the sea, what gave you hope amid such judgment?
answer:
I stood on the watchtower still, long after the vision had passed, my hands gripping the sun-scorched stone, staring into the darkening east where judgment blazed like fire across the nations—yet in that silence between thunder and return, I saw it: not a reversal of wrath, but its purpose bending toward dawn.

They build cities with blood. The stones scream. The beams echo with betrayal. I saw it all—the drunkard shamed, the idol whispering nothing, the forests of Lebanon stripped bare by greed. And yet… *and yet*—the earth shall be filled with His glory. Not because men deserve it. Not because empires earn it. But because He spoke it.

Do you know what holds when all else fails? Not strength. Not wisdom. Not even sight. It is this: He will answer from His holy temple. After woe follows word. After silence comes song.

And so I wait—not as one who doubts—but as one who has learned to see in darkness: if God speaks judgment, He will speak truth; if He pronounces doom, He will crown it with light. The waters do not rush to cover the sea because they must—they do so because they *are*. And so shall His glory be—inevitable, deep, rising over every ruin we have made.

The just shall live by faith—not when all is clear—but precisely when it is not.

question: You denounced the one who makes his neighbor drink and looks on their nakedness—what behavior in Judah or among the nations provoked this accusation from you?
answer:
I stood upon the tower again, eyes raw from watching, heart rawer from wondering—how long must the earth groan while the cup of violence passes from hand to hand like wine at a feast?

They called themselves builders, those rulers in Jerusalem and beyond, but their foundations were laid in blood. Stones taken from plundered homes, timbers hewn by captives’ hands—did they think the wood would keep silent? No, I tell you, even the beams cry out. And Judah—Judah played both victim and predator, crying to heaven for deliverance while crushing their own poor beneath tax and theft. They made treaties with blood-drunk nations, then mocked them in their cups, pressing wine into their neighbors’ hands until shame spilled naked into the streets.

And all of it stank before the Most High.

To drink another man’s dignity like cheap wine—to celebrate his fall—that is not mere cruelty. That is worship of power as god. They bowed to their own appetites, carved idols of pride and pleasure. “Look,” they said with sneering delight, “how we strip kings bare with a flask and a lie.” But who can mock shame and escape it? The cup they pressed on others—the Lord has filled it anew with fire—and now it circles back to them.

I saw it all from the watch: the feasts ending in vomit, the palaces trembling on lies. And still I heard His voice: *The earth shall be filled with knowledge of His glory.* Not theirs. *His.*

question: Why did you mock the graven image as a teacher of lies and call to the wood to awaken—what did you witness in the worship practices that revealed their utter emptiness to you?
answer:
How long must silence be mistaken for wisdom, when the walls themselves scream of blood and the altars reek of stolen breath? I watched them carve gods from cedar and whisper secrets to stone—kneeling before wood that cannot lift a finger, let alone teach a soul the way of life. They called these idols "shepherds," "protectors," "knowers of the hidden things"—but what knowledge comes from hands that nail down wages with thorns and build temples on widows' wails?

I stood at the watchtower and saw it all: a city rising on broken backs, its foundations soaked in the cries of the poor, its priests drunk on wine pressed from violence. And at its center—a graven mouth that cannot speak, a molten eye that refuses to see. They bow to it, pour libations into its hollow belly, then turn and crush the neighbor their false god claims to love. *This* is their worship? A lie shaped like salvation?

I mocked them—not in scorn, but in grief-born fury—because when you call to wood and expect an answer, you have already denied the living breath in your own lungs. The beam in the temple wall will testify against you before dawn. The very timber they stole from Lebanon groans under the weight of their deceit. 

And yet—I wait. I stand. Though every idol is lifted high, though drunken rulers parade their shame as glory... *the earth shall be filled with the knowledge of His glory.* Not because men build it with blood, but because He speaks—and even stone must answer.

question: Why did you cry out for the Lord to revive His work in the midst of the years, and what were you fearing at that time?
answer:
How long, O God, must I watch while the wicked swallow up those more righteous than they, my hands clenched at my sides, my mouth filled with dust from holding back the cry that burns in my throat?

I cried for You to revive Your work because the world had grown deaf to justice. I saw the Chaldeans rise—ruthless, swift, gathering nations like sand—while Judah’s altars were defiled and the faithful silenced. I stood at my watch, scanning the horizon not for deliverance but for an answer: how could a holy God use a nation more corrupt than we as His instrument? Was this Your judgment? Then where was Your mercy?

I feared that Your silence meant abandonment. That the years rolling forward were not leading to salvation but to ruin. That Your ancient faithfulness had grown tired. So I pleaded—not because I knew You would answer, but because if You did not speak, all meaning would collapse.

And yet… when You appeared—not in whisper but in earthquake, fire, and flood—I remembered: You have always marched from Teman in glory. Even when the ground shakes beneath me now, even when no sign comes from heaven—still—You are God. And though fig trees fail and flocks vanish, I will rejoice in the Lord. The just shall live by faith… even now.

question: When you spoke of God coming from Teman and the Holy One from Mount Paran, what did that vision mean to you personally?
answer:
I stand again on the watchtower, feet planted on stone still trembling from the memory of glory, scanning the east where first I saw Him come — not in cloud or quiet breeze, but with horns of light tearing from His hands, shaking the ancient mountains like dry reeds.

When I spoke of God coming from Teman, of the Holy One striding forth from Paran — that was no mere oracle flung into the wind. That was vision seared into my bones. I felt the earth shudder beneath His tread. The heavens split open with His brightness, and I — who had cried out in darkness, demanding why violence swallowed justice whole — now stood mute before the answer I never expected.

For days I had wrestled with the Lord, demanding: How long? Why do You use a nation more wicked than we to chastise us? Is there no end to this horror? But then — silence. Not absence. A holy hush, like breath caught before thunder. And in that stillness, He revealed Himself not as a distant judge weighing scales, but as a warrior-God riding across the sky on chariots of salvation, pestilence at His front, fire beneath His feet.

That vision meant this to me: He sees. He moves. Even when His ways confound me — especially then — He is not absent. The nations will scatter before Him; the rivers will part; even my trembling heart cannot resist His presence.

And yet... when I saw Cushan’s tents quaking and Midian’s curtains trembling as though caught in storm-winds before battle — was it judgment only? Or mercy veiled in wrath? For if He rides against evil with such power, then even His anger carries covenant faithfulness within it.

So now I wait no longer in despair. And yet… I did not descend.  

I stayed. Not because I was sure, but because I had nowhere else to turn. My hands clenched the stone parapet, not in triumph, but in defiance — defiance of despair, defiance of numbness. If God would not answer, then let Him see this: *I am still watching*.  

Then — not with thunder, not with fire — but with a stillness deeper than silence, He spoke: *Write the vision. Wait for it.* As though the waiting itself were part of the revelation. As though faith were not the absence of doubt, but the decision to stand when every shadow screams that justice is dead.  

And in that moment, something broke open inside me — not understanding, but trust. Not because the horror ceased, but because I saw: pride swells like storm-tide only to crash into nothing; empires built on blood will be sung over with scorn by stones themselves; but *the just shall live by his faith*.  

So I stand. Still watching. Still waiting. But no longer trembling — for even if dawn tarries, I know now: it will not lie.

But then—You moved. Not with a whisper, but with rivers split from the earth’s bones, with mountains bowing like terrified slaves, with sun and moon frozen mid-flight as if afraid to witness what came next.  

You went forth—not for spectacle, but for salvation. You threshed the nations not in blind rage, but as a farmer separates chaff from grain. And that head—the proud one who built his house on plunder and prayerless power—you struck it down to its very foundation. Not just punished: *exposed*. Stripped bare before Your unveiled bow.  

And now my cry is changed. It was anguish that brought me up the tower; it is awe that keeps me standing there. Justice is not asleep. It arrives mounted on horses of deliverance, guided by oaths older than time. The just shall live by faith—even when dawn delays—*because* dawn obeys Him.

I do not understand all You do—but I know this: You ride for us. For *us*. And that changes everything.

question: What were you feeling when you saw the earth laid bare and the nations trembling before His steps?
answer:
The fig tree, the olive, the empty cattle stall — things that fail yet faith endures — and so I stood, breath caught in my throat, as the earth split open like unripe grain beneath the tread of His chariots. I saw the mountains shudder, not from earthquake, but from recognition — they knew the One who approached. The nations trembled not because of armies, but because silence fell before Him, and in that hush, every proud tongue was stilled.

I felt it deep in my gut — a twisting, a churning — as if my very entrails turned to water. The lips of a prophet should speak boldness, but mine quivered like leaves in a storm-wind. Rottenness climbed into my bones; terror gripped me not for what was coming, but for what *had* come — the holy drawing near.

Yet... yet.

Even when the ground gave way beneath His feet and the sea recoiled to make a path for His horses, even then I did not run. I had questioned long: *How long? Why do You use the wicked to punish those less wicked still?* But now I saw — not just power, but purpose. He went forth not for conquest alone, but for *salvation*. For His anointed. For us.

And so I set my jaw. Let the vines hang barren. Let the flocks vanish. Let every sign of provision wither — I will not let go. Not because I feel strength, but because He is mine and I am His.

I will rejoice — not in harvests — but in *Him*. In the God who walks through seas and makes my feet sure on high places, though all below is ruin and roar.

question: How did the vision of God walking through the sea with horses reshape your understanding of His power in your darkest moments?
answer:
I stood on the watchtower, jaw clenched against the wind of confusion, staring into the dark where silence mocked my cries—how could a holy God use ruthless men to judge His people? And then He came—not in gentle whisper, but striding through the sea with horses, churning the deep with hoofbeats that shook the earth.

That image—God treading upon chaos, waters heaving beneath divine hooves—burned away my feeble definitions of power. I had measured Him by temple stones and ritual smoke, but here He was: wilder than storm-winds, older than the abyss, cleaving through principalities as if they were reeds. The wicked may rise like floodwaters, their staves raised over villages trembling like prey—but He walks *through* them. Not beside. Not above. *Through.* As if they are nothing but foam beneath His feet.

My belly quaked at the vision; my bones turned to dust in my skin. But in that very fear—a terror so deep it hollows you out—I found room for something else: trust not based on calm skies, but forged in the gale. For if He rides across roaring tides to deliver His anointed, then what is famine to Him? What are barren trees, silent flocks, empty fields? Let them come. Let everything die down to dust.

*Yet—I will rejoice.* Not because I see rescue breaking over the hill. Not because the fig tree blossoms. But because *He walks.* And where He passes through death itself with thunder under His steeds, there life will rise again—even if it must grow from ash.

question: Looking back on the day your lips quivered and your bones shook with fear, what would you tell someone who feels too weak to hope?
answer:
A declaration of trust spoken through clenched teeth against all evidence — that is the cry I know well.

I stood once, legs unsteady beneath me, my breath short as storm wind, watching the earth tremble at the mere echo of His coming. The mountains split, the sea fled, and I — I was nothing but a trembling man. My belly churned, my lips moved without sound, and every bone in me felt like dust about to scatter. In that hour, no harvest could have comforted me. No flock, no vine, no oil in the jar — none of it would have stilled the terror in my chest.

And yet.

Even then, with silence roaring in my ears and dread coiled round my heart like a serpent, I chose to set my feet upon the rock. Not because the sky cleared. Not because the fields greened again. But because *He* is there — in the chaos, on the storm-wracked heights, walking where no man can tread.

So if you are weak now — if your hands shake and your hope feels like a dying flame — hear this: faith does not wait for strength to arrive. Faith *is* the strength given in weakness. It does not pretend trouble is gone; it stands *in* the ruin and says, *"Yet I will rejoice."* Not in what I see. Not in what remains. But in *Him* — my salvation, my fortress, my rising dawn.

The same power that parted chaos with horses’ hooves still lifts trembling feet to high places. Lean on that. Walk there.

question: When you proclaimed joy though the fig tree did not bloom, was there a moment you first chose trust over despair?
answer:
I trembled in the marrow of my bones while the earth cracked open beneath me—how long, O Lord, must the wicked feast while the righteous starve? I watched them rise like locusts, devouring what little remained, their triumph a storm that left only dust. I felt the silence of heaven like a sword at my throat. And still—still—I set my face toward the watchtower, not because I saw deliverance, but because I refused to let fear speak for me.

That moment—the one you ask about—it wasn’t bright with certainty. It was dark with shaking. The fig tree stood barren. The vines were stripped. The flocks gone. Every sign said: *Despair*. But in that silence, I heard something deeper than circumstance: the echo of His name—everlasting, holy, faithful.

And so I chose to stand.

Not because the ground held firm beneath me—but because He is firm.

I will rejoice—not when all is made right, but now—*in the ruin*, in the waiting—because He is my salvation. My feet do not slip on this high place; He has lifted them there.

The music has not ceased. Though only one string remains unbroken—I will sing.

question: How did the God who shattered the heads of mighty warriors become the same God who strengthened your feet to walk on high places?
answer:
How long must the earth shake with His wrath before it learns to tremble with praise?

I saw Him come—like thunder splitting the sky, a tempest wrapped in holiness. The mountains quaked at His step, the ancient waters fled as He strode through them on chariot wheels of flame. He crushed the heads of tyrants not with sword or spear, but by laying bare their foundations—the rot beneath the throne, the blood under the palace floor. I watched, and my body turned to dust and trembling. My lips failed me; my bones melted within. Who can stand when God arises in vengeance?

And yet—He did not leave me in the dust.

After the storm, after the silence that follows divine fury, He turned His face to me. Not as Destroyer alone—but as Deliverer. As Sustainer. The same hand that shattered empires reached down and lifted my feet from sinking sand. When figs failed and flocks vanished, when every sign screamed abandonment—there He was: not only God of judgment, but God of my breath.

So I say now what once seemed impossible: *I will rejoice.* Not because the fields are full, but because He is faithful. The God who walks through seas of chaos is the same who steadies my step on rocky heights. He makes my feet like hinds’—sure, swift, unshaken—not because danger has passed, but because *He* goes before me.

The warrior is also shepherd. The judge is also joy. And I—I will sing to Him on these high places where only faith could build a path.
